mnemonics: Bayes' Theorem is like an UPDATE button: It takes your PRIOR belief about something and UPDATES it with new EVIDENCE to get a POSTERIOR belief.
Remember the formula P(A|B) = [P(B|A) * P(A)] / P(B) as 'Probability of A given B equals the Probability of B given A times the Prior of A, all over the Probability of B.'
keyPoints: Bayesian estimation treats parameters as random variables with probability distributions (priors), unlike classical statistics.
The Prior Distribution (P(θ)) represents belief about a parameter before seeing the data.
The Posterior Distribution (P(θ|X)) is the updated belief about the parameter after incorporating the evidence from the observed data (the likelihood).
Bayes' Theorem is the mathematical mechanism (P(θ|X) ∝ P(X|θ) * P(θ)) that combines the likelihood and the prior to form the posterior.
The Posterior Mean is often used as the Bayesian point estimate, which is an average weighted by the posterior distribution.
commonMistakes: Confusing the Prior (P(θ)) with the Likelihood (P(X|θ)): The prior is about the parameter, the likelihood is about the data.
Treating the prior as a single fixed value instead of a full probability distribution representing uncertainty.
Forgetting that the denominator in Bayes' Theorem (P(X)) is a normalizing constant that ensures the posterior is a valid probability distribution.
Using an overly confident (too narrow) prior that dominates the actual data, leading to a biased posterior.
Assuming Bayesian and classical (frequentist) estimates will always be the same; they are philosophically different and can yield different results, especially with small sample sizes.
